title: alnus glutinosa
intro: Alnus glutinosa, also known as the black alder or common alder, is a deciduous tree native to Europe, western Asia, and North Africa. The bark and leaves of the black alder have been used in traditional medicine for their anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can help to treat wounds, reduce fever, and alleviate respiratory issues such as bronchitis and coughs. The medicinal properties of Alnus glutinosa have been attributed to its high content of flavonoids, tannins, and essential oils, which contribute to its health benefits, including reducing inflammation, improving cardiovascular health, and acting as an antioxidant. Alnus glutinosa can be prepared in various herbal forms, including teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to treat colds, fever, and respiratory infections.
intro_study: According to European review for medical and pharmacological sciences, Alnus glutinosa has medicinal purposes related to its antimicrobial activity, which may be one of the reasons for its usage in treating wound healing in folk medicine.
benefits_desc: Alnus glutinosa is used to treat a variety of health issues due to its numerous benefits. The plant has been traditionally used to reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms associated with arthritis, gout, and other joint-related problems. It also exhibits antioxidant properties, which can help to protect against cell damage and promote overall well-being, in addition to its ability to lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels. Furthermore, Alnus glutinosa has been found to have antiseptic and antibacterial properties, making it effective in treating wounds, preventing infections, and even aiding in the management of diabetes and cardiovascular disease.

benefits_list.name: purification of water
benefits_list.desc: the roots of alnus glutinosa have the ability to purify water by absorbing heavy metals and other pollutants, making it a valuable tool for water filtration.

benefits_list.name: soil stabilization
benefits_list.desc: the roots of alnus glutinosa also help to stabilize soil, preventing erosion and landslides by holding the soil in place with their extensive root system.

benefits_list.name: habitat creation
benefits_list.desc: alnus glutinosa provides habitat for various species of insects, birds, and small mammals, playing a crucial role in maintaining biodiversity.

benefits_list.name: carbon sequestration
benefits_list.desc: like other trees, alnus glutinosa absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and stores it in its biomass and soil, contributing to climate change mitigation.

benefits_list.name: phytoremediation
benefits_list.desc: alnus glutinosa has the ability to remove pollutants from the soil and air, making it a useful tool for phytoremediation and cleaning up contaminated sites.

benefits_list.name: shade and windbreak
benefits_list.desc: the dense foliage of alnus glutinosa provides shade and acts as a windbreak, protecting crops and other vegetation from harsh weather conditions.

benefits_list.name: timber and fuel
benefits_list.desc: the wood of alnus glutinosa is valued for its durability and is used for timber and fuel, providing an important source of income for local communities.

actions_desc: Alnus glutinosa has been found to possess a range of therapeutic actions, including anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, antifungal, and astringent properties that can affect the cardiovascular system by reducing inflammation and improving blood vessel function, as well as the digestive system by soothing gastrointestinal issues and regulating bowel movements. Additionally, this plant exhibits antiviral and immunomodulatory effects, which can impact the immune system by stimulating its response to infections and modulating its activity. The antispasmodic and nervine actions of Alnus glutinosa also make it beneficial for the nervous system, as it can help to reduce anxiety and stress while promoting relaxation and calmness. Furthermore, its diuretic and expectorant properties can affect the urinary system and respiratory system, respectively, by increasing urine production and relieving respiratory congestion.

actions_list.name: demulcent
actions_list.desc: the alnus glutinosa plant has demulcent properties, which help soothe and protect mucous membranes, making it useful for treating skin irritations and wounds.

actions_list.name: anti-inflammatory
actions_list.desc: the plant has anti-inflammatory properties, which help reduce swelling and pain, making it useful for treating conditions such as arthritis and sprains.

actions_list.name: antiseptic
actions_list.desc: the alnus glutinosa plant has antiseptic properties, which help prevent the growth of bacteria and other microorganisms, making it useful for treating wounds and preventing infection.

actions_list.name: astringent
actions_list.desc: the plant has astringent properties, which help constrict and tighten tissues, making it useful for treating conditions such as hemorrhoids and diarrhea.

actions_list.name: expectorant
actions_list.desc: the alnus glutinosa plant has expectorant properties, which help loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, making it useful for treating respiratory conditions such as bronchitis and asthma.

actions_list.name: hemostatic
actions_list.desc: the plant has hemostatic properties, which help stop bleeding and promote clotting, making it useful for treating conditions such as nosebleeds and heavy menstrual bleeding.

actions_list.name: antioxidant
actions_list.desc: the alnus glutinosa plant has antioxidant properties, which help protect cells from damage caused by free radicals, making it useful for treating conditions such as aging and oxidative stress.

constituents_desc: Alnus glutinosa has been found to contain a rich array of bioactive constituents, including flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, which exhibit potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant also contains tannins, particularly gallic acid and ellagic acid, that have been shown to possess antimicrobial and antiproliferative activities. Additionally, Alnus glutinosa is rich in phenolic acids, including salicylic acid and ferulic acid, which have been demonstrated to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. Furthermore, the plant contains various alkaloids, such as berberine and saponins, which have been found to exhibit antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties.

constituents_list.name: tannins
constituents_list.desc: polyphenolic compounds that contribute to the plant's astringent taste and have antioxidant properties.

constituents_list.name: flavonoids
constituents_list.desc: a class of plant compounds with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, involved in plant defense against pathogens.

constituents_list.name: saponins
constituents_list.desc: bitter compounds that can produce a soap-like effect in water, and may have medicinal and antifungal properties.

constituents_list.name: phenolic acids
constituents_list.desc: a type of polyphenol with antioxidant properties, found in the plant's leaves and bark.

constituents_list.name: alkaloids
constituents_list.desc: a diverse group of compounds with pharmacological and toxicological properties, found in the plant's leaves and seeds.

constituents_list.name: glycosides
constituents_list.desc: compounds that consist of a sugar molecule bonded to a non-carbohydrate molecule, found in the plant's roots and leaves.

constituents_list.name: terpenoids
constituents_list.desc: a class of compounds with aromatic and medicinal properties, found in the plant's leaves and bark.

parts_desc: The most commonly used parts of Alnus glutinosa are the bark, leaves, and roots. The bark is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions such as eczema and acne, while also being used as a natural antiseptic and anti-inflammatory agent. The leaves are used to treat digestive issues like constipation and diarrhea, as well as being used to lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels, and even as a natural remedy for arthritis and gout. The roots of the Alnus glutinosa plant are used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma, and also have been used traditionally to treat infections, wounds, and even snake bites.

parts_list.name: leaves
parts_list.desc: the leaves of alnus glutinosa are dark green, lance-shaped, and 5-10 cm long. they are alternate and have a double-toothed margin.

parts_list.name: calyx
parts_list.desc: the calyx of alnus glutinosa is a green, 2-part, cup-shaped involucre that protects the flowers.

parts_list.name: cone scales
parts_list.desc: the cone scales of alnus glutinosa are small, rounded, and waxy.

parts_list.name: stem bark
parts_list.desc: the stem bark of alnus glutinosa is smooth and grey, but it becomes rough and brown with age.

parts_list.name: roots
parts_list.desc: the roots of alnus glutinosa are taproots that can grow up to 2 m long and are covered in a thick layer of cork.

parts_list.name: catkins
parts_list.desc: the catkins of alnus glutinosa are drooping, yellowish-green, and 2-5 cm long. they are male or female and are borne in clusters.

parts_list.name: nuts
parts_list.desc: the nuts of alnus glutinosa are small, 1-2 cm long, and are enclosed in a woody, 3-4 cm long cupule.

preparations_desc: Alnus glutinosa most common herbal preparations are infusions, decoctions, and tinctures. The infusions, also known as teas, are often used to alleviate digestive issues, such as bloating and constipation, and are also consumed to lower cholesterol levels and blood pressure. Alnus glutinosa tinctures, on the other hand, are commonly used as a natural remedy for respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and asthma, and are also taken to boost the immune system and fight off infections. Additionally, the plant's decoctions are traditionally used to treat skin conditions, such as acne and eczema, and are also consumed to aid in weight loss and improve liver function.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa infusion
preparations_list.desc: a tea made from the leaves of the alnus glutinosa plant, traditionally used to treat respiratory issues and as a diuretic.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa tincture
preparations_list.desc: a concentrated liquid extract of the alnus glutinosa plant, often used in homeopathy and traditional medicine to treat a range of issues, including digestive problems and skin conditions.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa salve
preparations_list.desc: a topical ointment made from the bark or leaves of the alnus glutinosa plant, traditionally used to treat skin issues, such as wounds and inflammation.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa decoction
preparations_list.desc: a strong tea made from the bark of the alnus glutinosa plant, traditionally used to treat digestive issues and as a diuretic.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa essential oil
preparations_list.desc: a concentrated oil extracted from the leaves or bark of the alnus glutinosa plant, often used in aromatherapy to promote relaxation and reduce stress.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa poultice
preparations_list.desc: a topical paste made from the crushed leaves or bark of the alnus glutinosa plant, traditionally used to treat skin issues, such as wounds and inflammation.

preparations_list.name: alnus glutinosa infused oil
preparations_list.desc: a carrier oil infused with the leaves or bark of the alnus glutinosa plant, often used in skincare and haircare products to promote healthy skin and hair.

side_effects_desc: Alnus glutinosa side effects are diverse and can be particularly bothersome for individuals who ingest its parts in large quantities. Common side effects include digestive issues such as diarrhea, nausea, and stomach cramps, as well as allergic reactions like hives, itching, and difficulty breathing. Those who are most likely to experience these side effects are individuals with pre-existing liver conditions, pregnant women, and children under the age of five due to their sensitive physiology. Precautions should be taken to avoid over-ingestion, as excessive consumption can lead to more severe side effects like seizures, tremors, and in rare cases, kidney damage.

side_effects_list.name: skin irritation
side_effects_list.desc: contact dermatitis and skin irritation may occur due to the allergenic properties of alnus glutinosa.

side_effects_list.name: gastrointestinal upset
side_effects_list.desc: ingestion of alnus glutinosa may cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea due to the presence of tannins and other toxic compounds.

side_effects_list.name: respiratory problems
side_effects_list.desc: inhalation of alnus glutinosa pollen or spores can trigger respiratory issues such as asthma, allergic rhinitis, and bronchitis.

side_effects_list.name: allergic reactions
side_effects_list.desc: some individuals may experience severe allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis, after exposure to alnus glutinosa.

side_effects_list.name: eye irritation
side_effects_list.desc: the sap of alnus glutinosa can cause eye irritation, redness, and tearing due to the presence of allergenic compounds.

side_effects_list.name: cardiovascular issues
side_effects_list.desc: long-term consumption of alnus glutinosa may lead to cardiovascular problems, including hypertension and arrhythmias, due to the presence of toxic compounds.

side_effects_list.name: neurological problems
side_effects_list.desc: ingestion of alnus glutinosa may cause neurological symptoms, including headaches, dizziness, and seizures, due to the presence of toxic compounds.
